text: This is an interesting laparoscopic splenectomy for ITP. The patient is a sixty-three year old healthy male.

The surgeon begins the laparoscopic splenectomy by mobilizing the splenic flexure with the patient in the left decubitus position. The lesser sac is opened anterior to the pancreas, which appears atrophic and medially placed, revealing the splenic hilum. The surgeon focuses on narrowing in on the splenic pedicle.

The surgeon encounters nuisance bleeding during the laparoscopic splenectomy procedure, with visible blood oozing from the surgical site. The patient's platelet count is noted to be in the fifties, potentially affecting hemostasis.

The surgeon manually controls the bleeding area and places Surgicel to manage the situation. Despite the presence of red bleeding, the surgical field remains well-lit with preserved colors, enhanced by the use of a 4K optical system.

The surgeon mobilizes the short gastric vessels and further opens the lesser sac, revealing intra-abdominal fat that slightly obscures visibility. The surgeon prepares to fire a stapler across the splenic pedicle, exercising caution due to the patient's history of ITP to avoid damaging the spleen.
The surgeon introduces the stapler, positioning it carefully across the splenic pedicle. As the stapler is fired, massive bleeding occurs, visibly complicating the surgical field.

The surgeon uses a grasper to gain control of the bleeding area, grasping tissue underneath the instrument. The bleeding source appears to be either the splenic pedicle or a large short gastric pedicle or branch.

The surgeon uses suction and additional graspers to gain control over the surgical field, avoiding the use of clips to maintain the ability to use essential devices like the Thunder Beat and staplers. The assistant's hand remains steady while the surgeon carefully dissects the short gastric and retroperitoneal attachments to the spleen.

The surgeon carefully reintroduces the stapler, passing it underneath the previously damaged area to control vascular inflow to the spleen. After closing the stapler, there is visible back bleeding from the vessel, which appears manageable. The surgical field is inspected for safety as the stapler is fired.

The surgeon places a stapler across the remaining tissue to complete the splenectomy resection. The surgical field is inspected, revealing no active bleeding, and clots are suctioned away. Clips are applied in various locations for safety, with visible red tissue but no active bleeding.
The surgeon places the spleen specimen into a retrieval bag and morselizes it through a fifteen millimeter trocar without any spillage.
